speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 6. The survivor is Mania Lichtenstein. Today is April 1, 1996. We ended the last tape with your statement, something happened, one year, one month, and one day. What was that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is when the third program took place. This is when they decided to eliminate the last 1,000 surviving Jews. And that was on a morning in December, '43? '43, I believe. Yeah. And it was unexpected. We were getting ready to-- for our daily work, going to the kitchen to get our coffee, when suddenly, there was chaos and tumult. And they dashed into the ghetto, the Ukrainians and the Germans, and immediately gathered up everybody-- almost everybody, because nobody expected anything. And it was the time when everybody was dressed and ready to start the day. It was terrible chaos, people running like mice, all over, not knowing where to run. Because in this ghetto, you really didn't have any planned hiding place. You kind of was resigned already.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nowhere to hide, really. Buildings were very primitive, you know? There was no-- there-- no-- it wasn't-- impossible to create some hiding place. So being right out in the street, or in the house, there was no problem for them to gathering up the 1,000. I, like among all the others, started running, not knowing where. Just like mice try to hide in any opening they can, I saw people running towards a little shed-- a little shed, a primitive little shed where you keep food or something. So I ran after them. It was a tiny little space. And we occupied it-- it was way above capacity, just as many as could run in, standing like-- like sardines, pressed together. you know? No-- no possibility for raising an arm or doing the slightest gesture. You were just-- as many people as could, just ran in, closed the door. How many people? Do you have an idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: At least-- at least, maybe, 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 20 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the capacity was like nothing, just a little shed. And we stand, and we listened to the horror outside. You know, we hear the screaming. We hear the shooting. We hear-- we-- we hear the panic. And we just stand there all day long. No food, no water, no movement, just stand. Anyway, all day long, the shooting was-- they were searching. Because now, come the last-- the last stage, where they accomplished-- the mission would be accomplished, the last 1,000.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they searched intensely all over, all day long. Until, in the middle of the night, we suddenly heard that there was no-- no sound. It was so quiet, as if everything is now at a standstill. And we are just standing there, incapable of existing any longer. As a matter of fact, it started raining. Because all the vapor from our breathing in that little enclosure, we were-- we were getting showered from our own breath. It was just physically unbearable. I mean, how long can you stand like this, pressed-- pressed together? Not even-- And we hear it's quiet, which means, mission accomplished. It's 1,000 only. So [SIGHS] knowing that, existing like this, and-- I don't know, there was also such-- the instinct was to-- to run, to hide, to-- to forget about what happened so far. It's like-- it's like it's still one action, you know? You're still running, you're still whatever. You-- you can't stop now. You-- you-- it's like you're caught in a-- in a-- in a-- in the middle of-- of a turning wheel. You cannot stop. You have to run. It's-- it has to run its course. So we really did not-- like when you asked me, what did I feel, no-- no such place for feeling, for planning, for wishing, or-- so you hide, because this is the instinct. You have to hide. You have to-- and you don't want to be shot in the back while you run, so you run fast and you hide. Once it was quiet, what do we do? How can you just-- you can't exist here any longer. And the rain is coming more and more, you know? So we decided-- we, like the older, maybe. I can't include myself, because I was really-- I was the youngest, and protected, very much protected. I wasn't such a brave doer, like some were more. Who protected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. [LAUGHS] No. I say, like, when I was a kid at home, you know? I was the young-- I was the baby. Everything I said was funny. I could get away with anything. But some kids were tougher, you know? They could take care, even if they weren't older than I. But maybe they were braver. Anyway. It was decided that we had to escape from that little shed. Because either we'll die here, or they'll come back tomorrow. They'll get us. We have to take a risk. Now, the ghetto was already deserted. No more people there. They knew-- the reason they stopped the shooting, the-- the-- the hunting, is because they had their, approximately, 1,000 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, Germans know exactly what's what, you know? We just don't go at random. They know exactly what they dealing with. They had their people. And maybe the few who are hiding, eventually, they'll crawl out, because you need food, you need drink. And the Poles will very much oblige. They won't-- they won't have a hard time. The Poles loved to report, oh, we saw a Jew, and-- you know? So we-- it was decided that we are going to leave the-- the thing. There's nobody there. There was no reason for them to sit at the gate and watch if anybody escaped. It didn't pay any longer, you know? The few, they'll come out, you know? So we ran out, two-by-two. Every 10 minutes, two-- two people would leave the place. Do you remember who you ran with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with-- with Paper, the one-- the friend that-- that-- from my sister's in-laws' family, you know? That, from then on, we stuck together, because she didn't have anybody, I didn't have anybody. And we stayed like two sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, again, as I say, running where? From the-- the fire into the frying pan? Where shall I go? I don't know where to go. But she said that her parents knew that Polish woman who might help us. Because her parents left some valuables before the war, in case there ever be a need for them to use her help, you know? So she says, we'll go there. And then, we go-- two-- two wretched kids in the middle of the night. I'm afraid of a dog, [LAUGHS] like you wouldn't believe. And dogs are barking all over. And got-- and it was like 3 o'clock in the morning, you know? The city was asleep. Otherwise, you could not walk. And this woman lived way in the outskirts. And we come knocking on her door.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a very good heart. Her name was Janina Zawadska. I'm sure she doesn't live anymore, because she was a-- she was a sick woman, but a good heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says, oh, my god, what shall I do? How can she throw us out, you know? We come in the middle of the night, knocking on her door, knowing what's happening to the Jews, because they all knew. I mean, it was obvious. And she let us come in and hid us in a little alcove, little, little tiny. There was a bed and a feather cover. A feather, you know? And kept us under the cover.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and in those days, and in this place, there was no such thing, like privacy, like, you know, before you come, you announce, or you-- like it's nowadays. Anybody, any neighbor, can come and knock in the winter, anytime. There was no such thing, like locking a door.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suddenly, she started locking the door, because we were inside. And the neighbors, the Poles, were very suspicious. They knew what's going on with the Jews. Hey, maybe you're hiding a Jew. And they-- maybe you got rich. Getting jealous, you know? Maybe we got her with some treasures in order to hide us. So they started questioning. What's with you, you, Janina? What's going on? Why-- why do you-- are you hiding Jews? Why do you hide? You're closing-- She says no, no, no, no. Anyway, it wasn't-- it wasn't possible to stay in there, any more. How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- well, we stayed-- | don't remember, but a little-- just like maybe days. And eventually, she took us to the stable. She-- she put us in the stable. She had some horses there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed in the stable. That was January. Severe winter. Our clothing was like next to nothing-- whatever we had on our back. We didn't have a change of clothes, you know? And we stayed in that stable. And she kept that stable locked, of course. But it was-- another thing, this woman, she was so good-hearted. She had such a good heart. She pitied the German soldiers, that they were away from their families. And it's war. So she used to entertain them, sleep around with them, you know? And it becames-- and they were always coming drunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, they were fighting with her to see what's behind that curtain, that little-- it was Christmastime. And she had such a good heart that she had to feed them-- feed them. They were away from home. And it's Christmastime. So she had to-- she-- she-- she killed a pig, and she fed a group of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were so drunk. And they wanted to know, what-- what did you hide in there? I-- you know, it was so funny. Like now, in a situation like this, my heart would have stopped. Because, oh, here we are, under-- under the-- under the bed cover, you know, covered over our heads. I don't know, then, whatever. Whatever. Of course, we were afraid, very afraid. But she said, oh come on, don't be stupid. There's nothing there, you know? It's just my bed. And-- and she kind of managed to get them away. But situations like this became so terrible, so-- so frequent, that she started getting very nervous. And every day, she'd come home, and she, oh, my god, what am I going to do? She was a religious-- like she used to talk to God. What am I going to do? Because every day, they-- they would find Jews at the Poles. You know, some Poles, they-- you pay them well, and they hid you. Some just gave you away, you know? They took the money, and they-- they called the Germans. And what-- [INAUDIBLE] when-- when they found Jews, the Pole was shot too. So she knew, oh yes, they-- they shot the Jews and the Poles. What am I going--? It became nerve-wracking. It became impossible. And the neighbors, more and more, started prying. What is it? What-- what's going on? Do you have Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It became-- and then, another-- another thing, which I'm trying to make it short, but my stories are big stories. The situation between the Ukrainian and the Poles was very, very tense. They were slaughtering one another. They were never in-- living in harmony. And-- and now, with-- with the Germans giving them such a boost, you know, they became high-and-mighty. They started with the Poles. Anyway, slaughtering. So the Poles from the villages started running away, coming to the city. And again, it became even more dangerous, because they were living in her-- you know, in her house, or next to-- next to her. And she says, children I cannot keep you any longer. But she found out about the Polish [NON-ENGLISH] And she says, this is where I'm going to send you, through the woods. Like Hansel and Gretel, [LAUGHS] sending through the woods. What she did is she-- that she-- she confided. She had confidence in that peasant. And I don't know, she gave him something. But this was a poor woman, but she shared with us a little piece of bread, whatever the soldiers would bring, a little sugar. And she-- she gave that peasant a bottle of vodka and said-- because we had-- when we left in the horse-and-wagon, you know, we were dressed up, covered. And only our eyes, you know-- because there was a good excuse. It was winter, the most severe winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says, when you come to this sentry, when you come to that-- where the-- where the Germans are stationed, and they ask, hey, what do you have here? Before they ask, she says, you just get that bottle of vodka out and say, hey, comrades, here's a bottle of vodka. You must be dead frozen. And you know, before they could say, even, ask a question, say, you just give them that bottle of vodka. Say, oh, you poor guys. You must be stiff frozen. Here's a bottle of vodka. Warm up. And-- and they didn't-- there were only two kids, you know? Maybe if there'd be two men, that they would be more suspicious. Sure enough, no questions were asked. And we are free. We are out of the zone of-- danger zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we go in that horse-and--- the wagon. And it shakes. And it was quite-- I don't remember how many kilometers. But it wasn't like in the city. You had to go through the woods. It was already a trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in January of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Approximately. OK. In '45. Yes. And eventually, finally, we get to the woods. There, we meet quite a few other young-- men, mostly, young boys, rather. You know, I wonder if there were any women.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my little friend and I, we didn't want to, at first-- they-- they stayed in a stable, on their own. Find food, whatever they could beg from a peasant. I don't know what they ate. [LAUGHS] But we decided, my little friend and I, we'll go from house to house. This was Polish partisans, stationed. So the Poles were not out to kill Jews or to-- to bother themselves with this issue, because they were kind of protected. The Polish partisans were big heroes, you know? Like Poles were always patriotic, and-- so they are going to fight against the Germans, which are-- which was absurd, because what can they do against them? They have a rifle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we-- we come. And we did not want to sleep in the stable with all these young boys. We said, we'll go from door to door and ask if they, perhaps, need somebody to knit. They have a baby. Sure enough, we came to Esquire, like a fine family. They had a little boy. And we were here-- they accepted us, let us sleep on the floor on straw, which was very wonderful in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They-- yeah, they must have. But they are not going to kill us. Maybe-- maybe they were decent people, too. And-- and-- and here, there was no issue of the Jewish issue. There was the Poles fighting continuously with the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there, and they feed us. And-- and these were, like-- Poles are-- either they, like, very primitive, you know, like the [INAUDIBLE] but people have a wrong impression of-- the Ukrainians are very primitive, like real uneducated, but not Poles. But of course, there's certain groups. But the others are, like, finer people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we ate, like, everything, like-- this doesn't exist in English-- like, let's say, you'll say, a small potato, that is [NON-ENGLISH], like every-- diminutive. Everything was fine language. And suddenly, we eat [NON-ENGLISH] And they give us potatoes. They give us food, normal, real, real food. And that was good. So we stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Germans eventually came with their airplanes. You know, it wasn't like-- first, there were skirmishes, you know? But eventually, they came to final take place. And they knew about these partisans. And they threw a few bombs in that village. And we immediately knew that, hey, that's no good. Run in the woods. So we ran deep into the woods. And we begged for some bread, because a lot of the farmers, of the peasants, ran into the woods, too, with the cattle and the horses, because they knew they were going to bomb and destroy everything. But the Germans came again. And we knew-- they were circling. We-- we could hear the-- the planes above our heads, like bees, you know? And like the Jews, the group of Jews, said, he'll stay-- staying here is just senseless. We have to take a chance. And started walking out the woods. Also, we met a Russian dispatcher in the woods. You know, it was very close to the front line. We were-- we could hear the artillery. It was the front line. And they-- there was a dispatcher to help, to see, partisans, or whatever, you know? And he told us that-- where the Russians are stationed now, where they are now, at what point. And he says, the only way for you to try and survive is to start running toward the-- the Russian side, you know? And this is what we did. We divided-- the Jews-- the Poles stayed on there, and then, sure-- soon enough, the planes came and leveled everything, you know? But again, [LAUGHS] we got out. Did this partisan group have weapons and try to do any sabotage, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [INAUDIBLE] rifles. No, I-- I don't even know, because we weren't part of it. We were just the Jews. They didn't-- they let us hang around, because they had better things to do, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, they would ask, who wants to go to work? Everybody wanted to go, because you got something to eat. So they selected a few young, healthy one. They never came back, because they shot them too. It's not that the Poles, you know saved us. But they had more of-- Who did the Poles shoot? The Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Jews. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jews that volunteered to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. They never came back. They went, who wants to-- everybody wanted to go to work. Work means you'll get something to eat. No. And they never came back. But I haven't seen it with my two eyes, but there were reports that they found their graves, like-- you know? They-- they-- they-- they too, they did not-- did not need or want the Jews, but we came. And they had more important missions to do than starting up a-- you know, an issue with Jews. So they let them hang around. Figured, whatever happens to them--

speaker: interviewer
text: This group, did it have a special name of the resistance group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me see. No, it's just-- the-- the place, it was a-- Well, now I forgot. I have it written down. Oh, [? Beilein. ?] [? Beilein. ?] It was in the woods, the Ukrainian woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a leader? Do you know the name of the leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. But they were connected-- they were [NON-ENGLISH], you know? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] They were connected with Sikorski, I think, was his name. In London, they had-- the Poles had a-- like a resistance center. If you-- if you-- you could have come across, in one of the writings-- I know I did-- that they had, like, their representatives, like the Poles, in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's go back, that you were running.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were connected.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you were advised by the Russian dispatcher where the Russian front was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At that time, news were very positive. It was really encouraging, because we could hear the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the sound of war, we could hear. It was at our footsteps. And we knew, already-- like, we didn't have a radio or any media to tell us what's exactly happening, but, you know, rumors. Somebody, somewhere, knew. We knew that the Germans are now pushing-- that the Russians are pushing the Germans back east. And they are recapturing city after city. You know, the-- the Germans just came until Stalingrad. And there, there was a real resistance where the Russian army-- excuse me. really said, OK, that's enough. From here on-- And this is when the offensive started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans were dying like flies there, because it's just like in Napoleon times. They weren't prepared for the Russian winter. They had little-- the Germans-- little woolen coats. You know? The Russians were equipped with the hats, and the boots, and the-- and their heavy things. And their--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German, of course, the morality became that it was degenerating. They-- they lost all their hope. And-- and they were captured-- after city after city being pushed back-- and captured as prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Russians were pushing the Germans back west.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pushing the Germans continuously back west, west, west. And we were going to-- across, to meet them. You know? Wherever-- The first place--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were traveling east?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were traveling east, yeah. Where the Russians are still-- and eventually-- yeah, we-- we went-- you know it was, as I say, the front line bombs full of-- planes were flying. Every time a plane approached, we would camouflage, press against a tree, you know? And like, I'm part of the tree. And when it passed, we go on. The shoes were broken. My feet were bleeding. I mean, this is the Ukrainian woods. Beautiful, beautiful woods. I never saw something like this on this continent, since I came here. And pine needles-- the needles were stuck in that broken shoe. My feet were bleeding. And we were walking-- like, at-- at that time, I met my future husband there. It was I and that girl that I survived all this with. And my future husband and his brother, they were in the partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband-- he was a very caring man, like, you before himself, you know? He had to attend to everybody, himself last. He found, somewhere a rag. And I wrapped it in my feet, because they were bleeding for the needles. Continuous pine needles. Beautiful. In-- in normal times, it would be a beautiful sight. So you were escaping with your future husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were-- and more people, too. But we were like-- we kept together. If we found a potato, we would cook it in the-- in the woods, when we were still-- in an open fire, and cut it in four, you know? Like, we shared everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you met him while you were running east.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but he was from my hometown, mind you. Yeah. He-- like I knew-- like I said, the boys were-- Jewish boys in a separate school, Jewish girls in a-- so it's just that I lived on that street. So I passed by, and the boys would whistle, look out the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew one another, but we were very shy. And [LAUGHS] even, I-- I used to-- maybe I shouldn't tell it-- I was very romantic, so every-- every, maybe, two weeks, I was in love with somebody else, but God forbid he should know about it. It could be a movie star. It could be my teacher. It could be anybody. I don't know on what basis I was [LAUGHS] judging my love, but god forbid, it should-- it's secret. And that was enough, you know? You giggled. You tell yourself secrets.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very-- we weren't like nowadays, that-- so we knew about one another's existence, but I don't think ever spoke to one another. But once you--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you met up in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then-- you know, then, you-- you were just living human beings. And-- and you just stick-- stuck together, no matter what those-- you know, what sex you were. You were a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have some romance, or romantic relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. It-- it wasn't even like two-- no, no, no. It-- it was enough caring, you know? Anything you needed, it was shared with me, and with the other one, and with the brother. It could have been a boy in my place, sharing. We didn't know me, me mine, you know? It was always we. That's why, I-- when I talk, I always say we, we, we, because there's no such thing like I, until a certain point, maybe. But that's it. And then, it was we, only the few that survived. Because this was your immediate family. You had nobody else. Nobody had anybody else to think or care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you running together with this group through the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when we escaped from the woods--

speaker: interviewer
text: Going to the east.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yeah, that we escaped-- I believe that we left-- it was, maybe, March, because I remember getting so romantic. The birds were-- the woods-- you-- you get-- you got the lily-of-the-valley blooming. The violets are beautiful, like, really as it should be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, spring, and the sun is warm. So-- so I remember, it was spring, so it must have been March. By us, March, the calendar said spring. It was spring, you know? And you felt yourself falling in love already, with this gentleman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe he with me, you know? He-- and no, there was no time yet. No-- no place. No, it-- I can't explain it. And also, the way we were brought up. Even, maybe, you didn't let yourself think in such terms, you know? You're very modest, very modest. But we-- we go. We go. And we-- we go from village to the Russians. And then, it was still not-- not good. I mean, it was good we are free. Bombs are falling, but this is a-- they're falling on everybody. You know, it's war, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Russians tell you, when you met up with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians-- we thought-- I was even preparing a speech in my mind. And I was kind of shy. But I will have to, because they will simply put me up there-- tell me how you survived. You know, this is how I felt, because we came, like, from-- from a different planet. How did you survive such a massacre? But it wasn't so. The Russian were very-- they-- they hated us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They wouldn't kill us, because it was war. It was a bad, miserable war, and they were fed up. And they claimed that the Jews, whenever they can, they get out of their duty, you know? They say, Abrasha, they would refer to a Jew, like Abraham. Abrasha doesn't want to fight. So they were mad.
